source_title: #840: Bill Gurley — Investing in The AI Era, 10 Days in China, and Important Life Lessons from Bob Dylan, Jerry Seinfeld, MrBeast, and More

paragraphs.speaker: tim
paragraphs.name: Tim Ferriss
paragraphs.text: Welcome to the Tim Ferriss Show. My guest today is Bill Gurley, general partner at Benchmark. He's invested in companies like Uber, Zillow, OpenTable, and Stitch Fix. His new book is Running Down a Dream: How to Thrive in a Career You Actually Love.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: I brought this book, The Last Laugh by Phil Berger, about the world of stand-up comics. Jerry Seinfeld read it when he was young and uncertain about pursuing comedy as a career. This book profiled fifteen different comedians—Bill Cosby, George Carlin, Lily Tomlin—and granted him permission to go do this career that's not typical. When you go to college, they don't list stand-up comedian as something you can do. But this book served as something that granted him permission.

paragraphs.speaker: tim
paragraphs.name: Tim Ferriss
paragraphs.text: Let's start with some topical subject matter: AI bubble or not, and if so, what does that mean?

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: My partner Peter reminded me of a book by Carlota Perez, Technological Revolutions in Financial Capital. Every time there's been a technology wave that leads to wealth creation, especially fast wealth creation, that will inherently invite speculators, carpetbaggers, interlopers. If the wave is real, then you're gonna have bubble-like behavior. They come together as a pair. Anytime there's very quick wealth creation, you get a lot of people that want to take advantage. There's a real technology wave fundamentally changing the world, and there's also massive speculation simultaneously.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: Even the big players have decided to do circular deals. Microsoft invested in OpenAI, and OpenAI agreed to buy services from Microsoft. Amazon wanted Anthropic to spend money they didn't have, so they gave them even more money. NVIDIA's handing out money and agreeing to buy any services left over. If you were to say what's clean accounting, you wouldn't do these kinds of things. I'm particularly concerned for retail investors because there's a plethora of SPV vehicles where someone has an allocation and allows others to invest, taking a rake. Most of the people on that edge I would put in the category of interloper, carpetbagger.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: If I were doing angel investments, I'd try to find the intersection of people who are super curious and playing with these AI tools but bring a perspective from a particular industry that gives them an advantage. Maybe the smartest user of AI in their domain. The institutional investors have zero interest in non-AI deals right now. Zero. I don't care what field you're in—you should be playing with this stuff. It has the potential to impact your role and your career. The best way to protect against any risk of your career being eliminated from AI is to be the most enabled version of yourself you can possibly be.

paragraphs.speaker: tim
paragraphs.name: Tim Ferriss
paragraphs.text: You spent ten days in China over the summer. What was your experience? What did you see?

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: I've been about six times before. This time I toured six cities in ten days with my daughter, who's an Asian Studies major. I took two high-speed trains, toured the Xiaomi factory with their new car, the SU7. I wanted to get a feel for what's most recent there. China is misperceived in many ways. People use the word communism to infer top-down, state-run system, thinking of Russia with bad capital allocation and no innovation. The reality is far different. The provinces compete with each other—if the effective mayor does well, he has a chance to move up in the system. This leads to massive competition and hyper-innovation.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: I met Lei Jun, founder of Xiaomi, back in 2005. He's evolved into the Steve Jobs of China. Ten years ago he declared he was going to build a smartphone—Xiaomi is now the third largest manufacturer of handsets globally. Four or five years ago, at the exact same time Apple hinted they were interested in building a car, he said I'm going to build a car. In his 2024 company address, he talked about putting a note on any car in his parking lot that he'd never drove, asking each employee for three positives, three negatives, and to loan him the car. He drove 200 of his employees' cars. When you hear that kind of stuff, you wonder if anyone at Apple did that.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: What I'm trying to accomplish with Running Down a Dream: there are people out there—young adults, maybe even midlife career—who have this notion that they should be doing something else, but society has put them on a path that they just don't love. I want to help them have the confidence and permission to go chase this dream. Your ability to make connections, to gather information and learn on your own pace has never been better.
